✅ Keep torso upright — don't lean forward
commonMistakes: ❌ Going too deep — stop at 60-70 degrees, not full squat depth
❌ Dropping fast — the SLOW lowering is the therapeutic mechanism
❌ Avoiding mild discomfort — up to 5/10 pain during exercise is acceptable per the protocol
coreBracing: LIGHT (20%) — maintain upright posture

breathing.pattern: Inhale during the slow eccentric (lowering). Exhale returning to standing.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Upright posture, minimal spinal load. Safe for back issues.
injuryNotes.knee: This IS a knee rehabilitation exercise. Up to 5/10 pain is acceptable during the protocol. If pain exceeds 5/10, reduce depth.
injuryNotes.shoulder: No shoulder involvement.

purpose: Gold standard eccentric loading protocol for patellar tendinopathy. The decline angle increases patellar tendon load compared to flat squats.
whyForYou: Your left knee post-surgical rehab benefits from eccentric loading. This targets the VMO and patellar tendon specifically.
proTip: No decline board? Use a rolled-up towel under your heels or stand on a slanted surface. The angle matters.
source: Alfredson Protocol / Purdam Decline Squat Study / BJSM Tendinopathy Guidelines

name: Spanish Squat (Band Behind Knees)

prerequisites.notes: Patellar tendinopathy alternative to decline squat. The band creates a posterior pull allowing more vertical tibia.

progressionChain.unlockCriteria: 3x15 pain-free with moderate band tension

steps: Loop a heavy resistance band around a rack at knee height. Step into the band behind your knees.
Walk back until the band is taut. Feet shoulder-width, toes forward.
Sit back into a squat — the band pulls your knees back, keeping your shins vertical.
Lower to 60-70 degrees over 3 seconds. Hold briefly. Return to standing.
formCues: ✅ Sit BACK into the band — let it support your knee position
✅ Shins stay nearly vertical — this is the magic of the Spanish squat
✅ Upright torso throughout — don't fold forward
commonMistakes: ❌ Standing too close to the anchor — you need enough band tension to pull knees back
❌ Going too deep — same 60-70 degree limit as decline squat
❌ Letting knees drift forward past band — the band should keep tibia vertical
coreBracing: LIGHT (20%) — upright posture, minimal spinal demand

breathing.pattern: Inhale lowering. Brief hold at bottom. Exhale rising.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Very low spinal load due to upright posture and band support. Safe for back issues.
injuryNotes.knee: This IS a knee rehabilitation exercise. The vertical tibia reduces patellofemoral compression. Up to 5/10 pain acceptable.
injuryNotes.shoulder: No shoulder involvement.

purpose: Alternative to decline squat for patellar tendinopathy. The band provides a posterior force that keeps the tibia vertical, reducing patellofemoral compression.
whyForYou: Easier to set up than a decline board and arguably more comfortable. Great alternative for your left knee patellar tendon work.
proTip: Try isometric holds at various depths (30s at 30°, 30s at 60°) — isometrics have strong evidence for tendon pain reduction.
source: Tendinopathy Rehabilitation / Spanish Squat Clinical Studies

name: Serratus Anterior Punch (Supine)

prerequisites.notes: Scapular protraction strengthening. Targets the serratus anterior — the muscle that holds the scapula flat against the ribcage.

progressionChain.unlockCriteria: 3x15 without scapular winging

steps: Lie face-up holding a light dumbbell (or no weight) with one arm extended straight toward the ceiling.
Without bending the elbow, punch the weight toward the ceiling by protracting (rounding forward) the shoulder blade.
Hold the protracted position for 1 second — feel the serratus anterior engage below your armpit.
Slowly retract the shoulder blade back to the floor. Repeat.
formCues: ✅ The elbow stays locked — all movement comes from the shoulder blade
✅ Think 'punch the ceiling' — the hand should travel 2-3 inches upward
✅ Feel the muscle engage on the side of your ribcage below the armpit
commonMistakes: ❌ Bending the elbow — this becomes a tricep exercise, not serratus
❌ Using too much weight — this is rehab, 2-5 lbs is sufficient
❌ Not fully retracting between reps — the full protraction-retraction cycle matters
coreBracing: LIGHT (15%) — maintain neutral spine supine

breathing.pattern: Exhale on the punch (protraction). Inhale on the retraction.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Supine position. No spinal load.
injuryNotes.knee: No knee involvement.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Start with NO weight if labrum is sensitive. The protraction movement is generally safe for labrum issues as it creates space in the joint.

purpose: Strengthens the serratus anterior to prevent scapular winging and improve overhead shoulder mechanics. Foundation for pressing safety.
whyForYou: A strong serratus anterior keeps your scapula properly positioned during pressing — critical for protecting your left labrum.
proTip: Place your opposite hand on the serratus (side ribs below armpit) to feel it contract. If you can't feel it, you're pressing too hard.
source: Scapular Stabilization Research / Shoulder Rehabilitation Protocols

name: Thoracic Extension over Foam Roller

prerequisites.notes: Thoracic kyphosis correction. Opens the mid-back. Safe for almost all populations.

progressionChain.unlockCriteria: Full comfortable extension over roller at 3 segments

steps: Lie face-up with a foam roller positioned horizontally under your mid-back (just below the shoulder blades).
Cross arms over chest or hands behind head to move shoulder blades out of the way.
Slowly extend backward over the roller — let gravity open your thoracic spine. Hold 2-3 seconds.
Move the roller up one segment (toward head) and repeat. Work through 3-4 thoracic segments.
formCues: ✅ Only extend at the THORACIC spine — not the lower back
✅ Keep hips on the floor — don't bridge up
✅ Breathe deeply into the stretch — exhale to deepen the extension
commonMistakes: ❌ Extending at the lower back instead of mid-back — keep abs engaged to lock lumbar spine
❌ Rolling too fast along the spine — pause and extend at each segment
❌ Placing the roller on the lower back — ONLY upper/mid back
coreBracing: LIGHT (20%) — engage abs to prevent lumbar extension. The extension should happen at the thoracic spine ONLY.

breathing.pattern: Inhale to prepare. Exhale and relax into the extension. Deep breathing opens the ribcage.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Keep the roller at thoracic spine ONLY — never place on lumbar spine. Engage abs to prevent lumbar extension.
injuryNotes.knee: No knee involvement. Bend knees with feet flat for comfort.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Hands behind head position may be uncomfortable — cross arms over chest instead.

purpose: Opens the thoracic spine and reverses kyphosis. A stiff thoracic spine forces compensations at the lumbar spine and shoulders.
whyForYou: Thoracic mobility is CRITICAL for your back and shoulder. A stiff mid-back forces your lower back and shoulder to overwork — exactly what you need to avoid.
proTip: Do this before EVERY session. 2 minutes of thoracic extension prevents hours of compensatory movement.
source: NASM Corrective Exercise / Thoracic Mobility Research

name: Standing Hip Flexor March (Band)

prerequisites.notes: Standing hip flexor strengthening with band resistance. Targets hip flexors without impingement risk.

steps: Stand on one leg. Loop a band around the foot of the working leg and anchor it low behind you.
Brace core. Lift the working knee up toward chest against the band resistance.
Raise knee to 90 degrees hip flexion. Hold 1 second at the top.
Lower slowly against the band tension. Complete all reps, then switch legs.
formCues: ✅ Stand tall — don't lean back to compensate for the band pull
✅ Control the lowering phase — don't let the band snap the leg down
✅ Stance leg glute should be active — squeeze to stay balanced
commonMistakes: ❌ Leaning backward to lift the knee higher — stay upright
❌ Using momentum instead of controlled hip flexion
❌ Neglecting the stance leg — it's working too
coreBracing: MODERATE (30-40%) — brace to prevent lumbar extension during hip flexion

breathing.pattern: Exhale as you lift the knee. Inhale lowering.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Brace core to prevent lumbar extension. If back arches, regress to hooklying march.
injuryNotes.knee: Monitor for pinching at the front of the hip. Reduce range if impingement occurs.
injuryNotes.shoulder: No shoulder involvement. Hold a wall or chair for balance support.

purpose: Strengthens hip flexors with resistance for improved gait, stair climbing, and athletic performance. Targets iliopsoas without impingement.
whyForYou: Strong hip flexors support your walking, stair climbing, and sport activities. The band provides progressive loading without spinal stress.
proTip: Focus on the stance leg just as much — single-leg balance + hip flexor strength is a two-for-one exercise.
source: Hip Flexor Rehabilitation / Functional Movement Training
